metadata.title: The 12-Steps and 12 Traditions Glossary
metadata.description: Comprehensive glossary of terms from AA's 12 Steps and 12 Traditions, including archaic words, AA-specific concepts, principles, and proper nouns. All entries organized alphabetically.

A.A..name: A.A.
A.A..definition: Abbreviation for Alcoholics Anonymous; the global fellowship of people recovering from alcoholism.

abundance.definition: A very large quantity or plenty. A future of abundance is promised by progress.

acceptance.definition: The action of accepting; receiving something willingly. In AA, acceptance means three things: acknowledging your powerlessness over alcohol, believing a Higher Power can help, and tolerating things you cannot change (like other people's behavior). Acceptance is the opposite of fighting reality.

accomplishment.definition: The successful completion of a task. Accomplishing recovery through united effort.

accountability.definition: Being answerable for one's behavior; taking responsibility. Part of making amends.

achievement.definition: Successful accomplishment; reaching a goal. Achievement of sobriety is emphasized in AA as coming through the Steps, not personal willpower.

acid test.definition: A crucial or decisive test (originally from chemistry - acid was used to test if metal was genuine gold). In AA, the acid test of Step Ten is whether you can stay sober and live well under all conditions.

acquaintance.definition: Knowledge or familiarity with something. Most people lack even a nodding acquaintance with humility.

actors.definition: People who perform roles or act. Alcoholics sometimes minimize Step Five by admitting to being 'bad actors.'

admission.definition: The act of acknowledging or confessing something; declaring acceptance. Admissions of powerlessness and defeat are essential to beginning recovery.

advancement.definition: Moving forward; promotion or improvement. In spiritual and personal matters.

advantage.definition: Favorable circumstance or benefit; superiority. Taking advantage of recovery.

adversity.definition: Difficult or unfortunate circumstances. In adversity, faith sustains one through reliance on Higher Power.

affirmative.definition: Confirming; asserting 'yes'; positive action. Step Three calls for affirmative action to turn over will to God.

aggravate.definition: To make worse; to worsen. Character defects cause or aggravate pressing problems.

aid.definition: Help or assistance; providing help. Mutual aid among members.

alcoholic.definition: A person suffering from alcoholism; someone unable to control their drinking and for whom alcohol causes problems. The term also refers collectively to AA members - 'alcoholics' in recovery.

Alcoholics Anonymous.name: Alcoholics Anonymous
Alcoholics Anonymous.definition: The full name of AA; a worldwide mutual aid fellowship for recovering alcoholics, founded in 1935.

ambition.definition: Strong desire to succeed; a goal. Personal ambitions are set aside for recovery work.

amends.definition: Making right past wrongs through apology and changed behavior. Not just saying sorry, but demonstrating change and attempting to repair harm - central to Steps 8-9.

anger.definition: A strong feeling of displeasure or hostility. One of the negative emotions that create emotional hangovers.

angle.definition: A hidden reason or point of view. The common question, 'There must be an angle somewhere.'

anonymity.definition: The state of not being identified by name; the principle that conceals member identities at public level. 'Anonymity is the spiritual foundation of all our traditions.'

appeals.definition: Requests for help or sympathy. Too many personal appeals would overwhelm AA if known.

appearance.definition: The act of coming into view; the way something looks. When the Big Book appeared in 1939.

approval.definition: Official or informal agreement; acceptance. Corporations' approval enhances AA's credibility.

arteries.definition: Vessels carrying blood from the heart. Used metaphorically for AA's vital channels.

articles.definition: Written pieces in publications. Articles in church journals prompt inquiry.

as we understood Him.definition: Phrase allowing each person to define their conception of a Higher Power. Intentionally left vague to include atheists (group as HP), agnostics (unknowable HP), religious (traditional God), and all others.

assets.definition: Valuable resources or advantageous qualities. Instincts are great natural assets when properly directed.

assistance.definition: Help or support offered. Assistance in the recovery process.

attainment.definition: The act of achieving or reaching a goal. The attainment of greater humility is the goal of Step Seven.

attention.definition: Notice or focus of mind; awareness. Paying attention in meetings.

attraction.definition: The power to draw or appeal to someone. AA grows through attraction, not promotion.

attraction rather than promotion.definition: Growing through appeal and example rather than active marketing. AA's public relations principle.

authenticity.definition: Quality of being authentic and genuine. Genuine recovery versus false recovery.

authority.definition: The power to make decisions or enforce obedience. In AA, authority rests only with a loving God.

autonomous.definition: Self-governing; independent; having autonomy. Each AA group should be autonomous.

autonomy.definition: The quality of self-governance; independence; self-direction. A fundamental principle in Tradition Four.

backing.definition: Support or endorsement; being behind something. Having backing of fellowship.

balance.definition: State of being in equilibrium. Balance in all areas of life.

bankruptcy.definition: Financial or moral insolvency; complete failure. In AA, refers to the complete spiritual and personal bankruptcy that precedes recovery.

basis.definition: The main reason or underlying principle. Basis of AA recovery.

bedrock.definition: Solid rock underlying soil; a firm foundation. The foundation of recovery built on admitting powerlessness.

beginning.definition: First part or start; commencement. Beginning of recovery journey.

behavior.definition: The way one acts or conducts oneself. Drinking behavior must be examined and changed.

belligerent.definition: Aggressive, hostile, or ready to fight. Describes the combative stance some take against Step Two.

benefit.definition: An advantage or improvement; something helpful. Benefits of following Steps.

Big Book.name: Big Book
Big Book.definition: The primary AA text, officially titled 'Alcoholics Anonymous,' published in 1939. Contains the Twelve Steps and recovery stories.

blessings.definition: Benefits or gifts; divine favor. Material blessings from technology and industry.

board.definition: A group governing an organization. AA has no board of directors with power to expel members.

brotherhood.definition: Feeling of fellowship and kinship; bond between members. AA's community of recovering alcoholics.

bypass.definition: To go around; to avoid. Many try to bypass Step Five by making only partial admissions.

cancer.definition: A disease; used metaphorically for the human condition without spiritual principle. Metaphor for alcoholism.

care.definition: Serious attention or consideration; looking after. Care for fellowmembers.

careers.definition: Occupations or professions; courses of life. Step Five involves examining career mistakes and harms.

carry out.definition: To execute or perform; to put into effect. Praying for power to carry out God's will.

carry the message.definition: To bring the AA message of recovery to alcoholics. The primary function of AA service.

caution.definition: Careful attention to avoid danger; wariness. Exercising caution when making amends.

caves.definition: Large underground chambers. Reference to where alcoholics would be 'back again in their caves' without AA.

character.definition: The basic makeup of who you are - your habits, values, and how you treat others. The core of your personality.

character assets.definition: Positive qualities one possesses - honesty, courage, compassion, integrity, wisdom. Step work helps identify and use these strengths rather than focusing only on flaws.

character growth.definition: Development of positive character traits and virtues. Result of working the Steps.

charity.definition: Generous giving to help those in need; goodwill toward others. Service to suffering alcoholics.

child.definition: A young person; offspring. Anonymity was 'the child of our early fears.'

clarity.definition: Quality of being clear; lucidity. Clarity through recovery.

classes.definition: Groups or categories of people. Step Nine amends fall into several classes based on circumstances.

clergyman.definition: A man who is ordained as a religious minister or priest. Historical reference to spiritual authority.

clubs.definition: Organizations or gathering places. AA clubs serve as meeting places.

commencement.definition: Beginning or start; ceremony marking beginning. Commencement of recovery.

commitment.definition: Pledge or promise; dedication to a cause. Commitment to recovery and helping others.

committees.definition: Groups appointed or elected to perform specific functions. AA may establish committees for service work.

common welfare.definition: The well-being or good of the whole community. Should come first; personal recovery depends on it.

companionship.definition: Friendly association with others; the desire for company. A legitimate human desire when not misdirected.

compassion.definition: Sympathetic concern for others' suffering; empathy. The basis of service and helping others.

comprehension.definition: Ability to understand; grasping meaning. Comprehension of principles.

concentration.definition: Fixing attention; focusing on one thing. Concentration on Steps.

concern.definition: Worry or anxiety; care for something. Concern for others' recovery.

confidence.definition: Trust or belief in someone or something. AA's sacrifices give people confidence in the future.

conscience.definition: Internal sense of right and wrong; moral awareness. Part of 'group conscience.'

conscious contact.definition: Aware and direct connection or relationship. Step Eleven seeks to improve conscious contact with God.

consequences.definition: Results or effects of actions; the outcomes of drinking and dishonest living.

consideration.definition: Thoughtful care for others; taking into account. When making amends.

consistency.definition: Quality of being consistent; regularity. Consistent practice of Steps.

contempt.definition: Feelings of disrespect or disdain. Ex-drinkers feared contempt from society.

contentment.definition: State of being satisfied and at peace. From living by spiritual principles.

conviction.definition: Strong belief or firmly held opinion. Arrived at through reflection in Steps.

cooperation.definition: Working together for mutual benefit; teamwork. Essential in group function and recovery.

core.definition: Central or most important part. Core of the recovery program.

corporations.definition: Large business organizations. Great corporations have set their approval on AA.

cost.definition: Price paid; sacrifice required. Whatever personal cost, members must devote themselves to recovery.

courage.definition: The ability to face fear or difficulty; bravery in facing one's wrongs. Required for Steps Five and Nine.

Creator.definition: One who creates; in AA, referring to God. Growing in 'the image and likeness of his own Creator.'

creditor.definition: One to whom money is owed. In Step One, alcohol is described as a 'rapacious creditor' that bleeds those addicted of self-sufficiency.

cure.definition: A remedy for disease; restoration to health. AA found a cure for alcoholism.

darkness.definition: Absence of light; obscurity. Reference to being cast into 'outer darkness' by authoritarian organizations.

declining.definition: Refusing or rejecting; turning down. AA should decline outside contributions.

dedication.definition: Wholehearted devotion; commitment. Dedication to spiritual principles.

defeat.definition: To win a victory over; to overcome. Complete defeat is the beginning of recovery in AA.

defects.definition: Flaws or weaknesses in character. Not fatal flaws, but areas where spiritual growth is needed.

defects of character.definition: Character flaws or weaknesses that cause problems - like selfishness, dishonesty, fear, and anger. Not moral evil, but human weaknesses that need spiritual change.

deferred.definition: Put off to a later time; postponed. Some amends must be deferred if timing is not right.

deflate.definition: To reduce in size or volume; to reduce pride or confidence. Steps deflate the ego, which is necessary for recovery.

degree.definition: A unit of measurement; an extent or level. Even a small degree of humility is necessary for sobriety.

dependability.definition: Quality of being dependable; reliability. Becoming dependable in recovery.

dependence.definition: Reliance on another person or substance; being dependent. Alcoholism creates unhealthy dependence.

desire to stop drinking.definition: The sole requirement for AA membership. The only criterion for joining AA.

devastating.definition: Highly destructive or damaging; overwhelming. The devastating effects of alcoholism on self and others.

development.definition: Process of growing or improving. Spiritual development through Twelve Steps.

deviation.definition: A departure from an established course or standard. Every conceivable deviation from the Steps and Traditions has been tried.

devotion.definition: Love, loyalty, or enthusiasm for someone or something. Devotion to recovery.

dignity.definition: Worth and honor; quality of deserving respect. All have dignity regardless of past.

dilemma.definition: A situation requiring a difficult choice between equally undesirable options. The conflict newcomers feel about believing in a Higher Power.

direct amends.definition: Face-to-face restitution or apology to someone harmed. Distinguished from indirect or written amends.

direction.definition: Course or path; guidance. AA gets its direction from a loving God, not from human leaders.

directive.definition: An instruction or order. No AA member can give another a directive or enforce obedience.

directors.definition: People who supervise or manage. No board of directors with punishment power in AA.

discernment.definition: The ability to see and understand clearly. Spiritual discernment means seeing deeper truths beyond just facts.

discipline.definition: Training to ensure obedience; self-control. Self-discipline in recovery.

disclosure.definition: Making something known; revealing. Disclosure in Step Five.

distrust.definition: Lack of trust or confidence. Fear of public distrust and contempt.

divine guidance.definition: Direction provided by God or Higher Power. What AA seeks through prayer and meditation.

doctor.definition: A medical professional. Used metaphorically for those working in AA recovery.

dope addiction.definition: Addiction to narcotic or habit-forming drugs. Area where AA thought it might help but should not expand.

drinkers.definition: Those who drink alcohol; alcoholics. Problem drinkers that AA members help.

drinking.definition: The act of consuming alcohol; also refers to the disease of alcoholism and compulsive alcohol use.

dry drunk.definition: A person sober from alcohol but not working the spiritual principles; still behaving irresponsibly and selfishly. Sobriety without spiritual change; just stopped drinking, not recovered.

duty.definition: A responsibility or commitment; what one must do. Duty to fellowship and recovery.

effort.definition: Exertion of physical or mental power. United effort is required to accomplish the cure.

ego deflation.definition: The reduction of excessive pride or self-regard. Necessary for spiritual growth and recovery.

egos.definition: The self, especially as distinct from the world; self-esteem. All Twelve Steps deflate the ego, which is necessary for recovery.

emergency.definition: A serious situation requiring immediate action. Faith can sustain one through any emergency.

emotional hangover.definition: Lingering emotional distress from past emotional excess. Distinguished from physical hangover.

emotional problem.definition: Psychological distress or mental health issues. Nearly every serious emotional problem stems from misdirected instinct.

emotional sobriety.definition: Being mentally and emotionally stable and clear - not just staying sober from alcohol. Finding peace and balance in how you think and feel.

empathy.definition: Understanding and sharing another person's feelings. Feeling with someone.

encouragement.definition: Support and hope given to someone; words or actions that inspire. Uplifting support.

endorse.definition: To approve and support; to sign the back of a check. To back or advocate.

equality.definition: State of being equal; treating all with same respect and dignity. All AA members are equal.

equilibrium.definition: State of balance; stable condition. Emotional equilibrium through recovery.

essence.definition: The most important element; basic nature. Essence of AA principles.

esteem.definition: High regard or respect; value and appreciation. Self-esteem returns through recovery.

event.definition: An occurrence or happening. The event of public knowledge would harm AA.

evolution.definition: Gradual development or change. As person evolves through recovery.

exaggeration.definition: Representation of something as greater than reality. No exaggeration that publicity brings members.

excellence.definition: State of being outstanding or excellent. Striving for excellence in recovery work.

exert.definition: To apply effort or force; to make an effort. People must exert themselves to be secure and survive.

experience.definition: The accumulated knowledge and practical understanding from lived events. Experience is how AA members learn - 'Proved beyond doubt by immense experience' is the foundation of the program.

expression.definition: Communication of ideas or feelings; manifestation. God expresses Himself through group conscience.

face-to-face work.definition: Direct personal contact and interaction. Recovery work must be based on desire to help, not payment.

facts.definition: Things known to be true; reality. The facts of AA life are based on collective experience.

fairness.definition: Impartial treatment; lack of bias or favoritism. In dealing with all persons harmed.

faith.definition: Trust or confidence in a power greater than oneself; belief that recovery is possible through spiritual principles.

faithfulness.definition: Quality of being faithful; reliability. Faithful practice of Steps.

families.definition: Groups related by blood or marriage. Inquiries come from alcoholics and their families.

fear.definition: An emotion of dread or apprehension. Fear prevents many from taking Step Five and from trying new things in recovery. Different from self-centered fear.

fearless.definition: Honest despite fear of judgment or shame. Not lacking fear, but willing to face facts anyway. A necessary willingness to look at uncomfortable truths about oneself.

fellowship.definition: Friendly association with others; community of shared purpose. In AA, fellowship is both the feeling of belonging among people with the same problem and the practical support of attending meetings, having relationships, and being part of something larger than yourself.

Fellowship.name: Fellowship
Fellowship.definition: The AA community; the collective body of all AA members worldwide working together for recovery.

films.definition: Moving pictures or cinema. Anonymity in public media like films.

finance.definition: To provide funds or capital for something. AA should never finance outside facilities or enterprises.

firmness.definition: Quality of being firm; strength. Firm foundation in principles.

firms.definition: Business organizations. Employers in firms want to know about alcoholism treatment.

flourishing.definition: Thriving and developing well. How members flourish in recovery.

focus.definition: Center of activity or attention; concentration. Focusing on recovery work.

folk.definition: People or persons. Business and professional folk couldn't carry on occupations if known.

food.definition: Nourishment needed to sustain life. Harvesting food is a basic survival need.

foundation.definition: A base or groundwork. Anonymity is the spiritual foundation of all traditions.

freedom.definition: State of being free; liberty. Freedom from alcohol and its consequences.

fulfillment.definition: Completion or realization of something desired. Of purpose and potential.

functions.definition: Purposes or intended uses. Instincts have proper functions that go wrong when misdirected.

future.definition: Time that is to come. People have confidence in AA's future.

gain.definition: Something obtained or acquired; profit. Gains from recovery work.

gentleness.definition: Soft, mild, or tender quality. Approach to others in recovery.

genuineness.definition: Being authentic or real; genuine quality. Must be genuine in recovery work.

ghosts.definition: Haunting memories or regrets from the past. The 'tormenting ghosts of yesterday' that haunt alcoholics.

gift.definition: A present or offering; something given freely. AA's unity is God's gift to the fellowship.

god.definition: In AA, referred to as 'God as we understood Him.' Not necessarily religious; represents a power greater than oneself.

God as we understood Him.definition: A foundational phrase meaning each person can define God/Higher Power according to their own beliefs. The phrase allows atheists, agnostics, and religious members to all find their own understanding without requiring a specific deity belief.

God's will.definition: In AA, refers to the divine guidance or direction that comes from a power greater than oneself. Central to Step Eleven, which seeks knowledge of God's will for us and the power to carry it out. The direction or purpose that comes from surrendering to a power greater than oneself; accepting what the Higher Power intends for one's life rather than insisting on one's own desires.

grace.definition: Divine or spiritual favor; a gift from God. Life-giving grace of God flows through AA.

grasp.definition: Understanding or comprehension; firm hold. Grasp of recovery.

gratitude.definition: Appreciation for benefits received; thankfulness. Essential for acknowledging God's grace.

groundwork.definition: Preliminary work establishing basis. Groundwork of Step One.

group conscience.definition: The collective wisdom and decision-making of a group. The true authority in AA.

Group conscience.name: Group conscience
Group conscience.definition: The collective wisdom and decision-making process of an AA group; decisions made by consensus.

group purposes.definition: The specific functions and goals of an AA group. Self-support is necessary to handle group purposes.

groups.definition: Collections of people or things. Nameless groups of alcoholics were secret societies.

growth.definition: Process of increasing in size or development. Spiritual and emotional growth through Steps.

happiness.definition: A state of well-being and contentment. Real happiness comes from spiritual principles, not from drinking.

harmony.definition: State of agreement; working together without conflict. Desired state within AA groups.

harnessed.definition: Brought under control and put to use; channeled. Resources being harnessed to create material abundance.

harvest.definition: To gather or reap; to collect something grown. Must harvest food or perish.

haven.definition: Place of safety or refuge. AA as safe haven.

health.definition: State of physical and mental well-being. Recovery restores health to body and mind.

heart.definition: The center or core of something; symbol of emotion. The heart of AA would cease to beat without unity.

help.definition: Providing assistance or support. Helping others find recovery.

Higher Power.name: Higher Power
Higher Power.definition: In AA, any power greater than oneself that one relies on for recovery. May be God, nature, the group, or any spiritual concept.

hint.definition: A slight indication or suggestion. Even the hint of publicity shocked early AA members.

His will.definition: The will of God or a Higher Power, with 'His' capitalized to show reverence and recognition of a divine power. In AA, aligning with His will means accepting the Higher Power's guidance instead of relying solely on self-will. The intentions and direction of God or the Higher Power; accepting divine guidance as more reliable and beneficial than one's own willfulness.

homes.definition: Residences; places of dwelling. AA meetings often happen in members' homes.

honesty.definition: The quality of being truthful and sincere. In AA, honesty means telling the truth to yourself (about your drinking), to others (in Step Five), and in your daily life. It's the opposite of denial and the foundation of recovery - you cannot recover if you're lying about the problem.

hope.definition: Expectation of something good; confidence in the possibility of recovery. Necessary for taking the Steps.

hospital.definition: A facility for treating the sick. Example of enterprises AA considered but should not undertake.

humbleness.definition: Quality of being humble; modesty. Essential for accepting help and guidance.

humiliation.definition: The feeling of being embarrassed or disgraced. Part of the spiritual experience needed for recovery.

humility.definition: Modest or low view of one's importance; absence of pride. In AA, humility is not self-hatred but honest self-assessment - seeing yourself as you really are, neither grandiose nor worthless. It's the willingness to learn and be teachable, which is necessary to accept help and change.

ideal.definition: A conception of something in its most perfect form. Humility as an ideal is misunderstood in modern society.

identification.definition: Recognition of sameness; association with. AA's unique ability to identify with problem drinkers.

image.definition: A representation or likeness of something. Growing in God's image is part of spiritual development.

immense.definition: Extremely large or great; vast. References the immense experience and knowledge of AA's accumulated recovery.

importance.definition: Quality of being important; significance. Priority and importance of sobriety.

improvement.definition: Making or becoming better. Continuous improvement through Step Ten.

incentive.definition: Something encouraging action; motivation. Incentive to maintain recovery.

inception.definition: Beginning or start; originating. From inception of AA.

independence.definition: Freedom from depending on others; self-reliance. Healthy goal after recognizing interdependence.

information.definition: Facts or knowledge provided. Clergymen and employers seek information about AA.

initiation.definition: Beginning or start; introduction. Initiation into recovery.

injury.definition: Harm or damage caused to another person. Considering whether making amends would cause further injury.

inquiries.definition: Questions asked seeking information. Doctors, clergy, and employers make inquiries about AA.

inscription.definition: Words carved, engraved, or written as a permanent record. The pathway's inscription: 'This is the way to a faith that works.'

insight.definition: Deep understanding or perception; seeing into a situation. Gained through inventory work.

inspiration.definition: Stimulation of the mind or emotions; creative influence. Inspiration from members' stories.

instinct.definition: A natural, involuntary inclination to act in a certain way. God-given instincts (for survival, reproduction, companionship, security) are healthy when balanced, but become defects when excessive.

integrity.definition: The quality of being honest and having strong moral principles. Foundation for trustworthy recovery.

intelligence.definition: The ability to understand and reason. Intelligent scientists force nature to disclose her secrets.

interdependence.definition: Depending on each other in a healthy way. In AA, members need the group and the group needs each member - that mutual reliance is healthy.

interest.definition: Concern or attention for something. In one's own recovery and others'.

introduction.definition: Bringing in or presenting something new. Introduction to recovery.

inventory.definition: A complete list or assessment; in AA, a thorough examination of one's character, behaviors, and past wrongs.

jealousy.definition: Resentment of a rival or of someone's success. One of the negative emotions that must be eliminated.

John Barleycorn.definition: An old poetic personification of alcohol (from traditional English folk songs and literature, where John Barleycorn represents the grain - barley - that becomes beer). AA uses the term to describe alcohol as a tyrant or enemy with power over the alcoholic, not just a passive substance.

John Doe.name: John Doe
John Doe.definition: A generic or hypothetical person's name used as an example in AA literature to illustrate a principle.

journals.definition: Periodical publications. Church journals carry articles about AA.

judgment.definition: Ability to assess and form opinions; discernment. Sound judgment in amends.

justice.definition: Fairness and moral rightness; treating others as they deserve. Part of the amends process.

kindness.definition: Quality of being kind; considerate and helpful. How members treat each other.

knowledge.definition: Information or understanding; what is known. Praying for knowledge of God's will.

leaders.definition: People who guide or direct others. AA leaders are trusted servants, not rulers.

least possible organization.definition: Minimal structure and hierarchy. The original formulation of Tradition Nine.

legions.definition: Large groups or numbers. AA's legions of well-wishers have helped growth.

let go and let God.definition: Common AA phrase meaning stop struggling and trust the process/Higher Power. For non-religious: accept reality, stop resistance, trust in principles that have worked for others.

level.definition: A degree or stage; a plane of existence. Personal anonymity must be maintained at the level of press, radio, and films.

liabilities.definition: Legal or financial obligations; disadvantages. When instincts are misdirected, they become physical and mental liabilities.

liberation.definition: The act of freeing from restraint or oppression. Recovery is liberation from the bondage of alcohol.

lifted.definition: Raised or moved upward; removed or taken away. The obsession was lifted out of them by Higher Power.

likeness.definition: A copy, representation, or similarity. Growing in the likeness of one's Creator.

list.definition: A written enumeration or catalog. Step Eight requires making a list of all persons harmed.

living amends.definition: The ongoing act of staying sober and living by principles. The best amends one can make is becoming a responsible, honest person - proving through action that change is real.

logic.definition: System of reasoning; rational principles. Logic behind the Steps.

love.definition: Deep affection and concern for others; unconditional positive regard. Step Twelve's 'love with no price tag.'

love with no price tag.definition: Unconditional love given freely without expectation of reward. The essence of Step Twelve service.

loyalty.definition: Quality of being faithful and devoted. Loyalty to AA principles.

lucidity.definition: State of being clear-minded; sanity. Lucidity restored through sobriety.

majestically.definition: In a grand, impressive, or stately manner. Describes humanity's view of itself as evolved and superior.

manifestation.definition: A clear sign or display of something. How spiritual principles show up and work in real life.

maturity.definition: State of being fully developed; emotional and mental adulthood. Development through recovery.

meaning.definition: Sense or significance; what something means. Finding meaning in recovery.

meditation.definition: Focused thinking or contemplation; quiet reflection. Central to Step Eleven's approach to God.

meekness.definition: Mild or gentle nature; humble. Not weakness but strength under control.

membership.definition: State of being a member; collective members. Half of AA's membership came through publicity channels.

membership rules.definition: Formal regulations governing who can join. Early AA groups had many membership rules.

message.definition: A communication or information conveyed. Carrying the AA message to suffering alcoholics.

Middletown.name: Middletown
Middletown.definition: A hypothetical American town used in the text as an example of where AA members might move and establish new groups.

millennium.definition: A period of 1,000 years; an age of peace. A man-made millennium is falsely promised by progress.

misdirected.definition: Directed toward a wrong target or goal; guided improperly. When natural instincts are misdirected, they cause problems.

misunderstood.definition: Interpreted incorrectly; not properly understood. Humility is widely misunderstood in modern society.

moderation.definition: Avoidance of extremes; temperance. Living with moderation.

modesty.definition: Humble or modest behavior; lack of boastfulness. Related to humility in recovery.

moral.definition: Relating to principles of right and wrong behavior. Moral inventory means examining one's ethical failings.

moral improvement.definition: Enhancement of one's ethical conduct and character. Goal of moral inventory work.

moral inventory.definition: A thorough, honest written review of your past - what went wrong, why it went wrong, and your own role in it. Not a harsh judgment of yourself, but a factual look at assets and liabilities, like a business inventory.

motivation.definition: Reason for action; driving force. Motivation comes from seeing recovery work.

nature.definition: The natural physical world. Nature reveals her secrets to scientific investigation.

newcomer.definition: Someone recently arrived in AA, usually within first 30 days. Newcomers are treated with special care and are often paired with sponsors.

newspapers.definition: Publications containing news. Many came to AA through reading newspapers.

no opinion.definition: Refraining from taking a position. AA has no opinion on outside issues.

nonprofessional.definition: Not involving or requiring professional training or payment. AA should remain forever nonprofessional.

obedience.definition: Compliance with orders or rules. In AA, members are not forced into obedience to authority.

obligation.definition: A duty or responsibility. AA members are bound by obligation to help problem drinkers.

obsession.definition: A persistent, compulsive preoccupation with something. In AA, the mental obsession with drinking is a key symptom of alcoholism.

occupations.definition: Jobs or professions. Carrying on occupations required anonymity.

offices.definition: Places of business or work. AA offices, clubs, and homes are centers of fellowship.

ooze.definition: To slowly seep or flow; also refers to primordial matter from which life supposedly evolved.

openness.definition: Quality of being open; transparency. Openness to new ideas in recovery.

organized.definition: Arranged in a systematic structure with authority and rules. AA as a whole should never be organized.

origin.definition: Point of beginning or source. Origin of Twelve Steps.

outside contributions.definition: Money or support from sources outside AA. AA groups should decline these to maintain independence.

outside issues.definition: Political, social, religious, or other controversial matters outside AA's primary purpose. AA avoids these.

painstaking.definition: Showing great care and effort; meticulous. Step Four requires painstaking, honest examination.

papers.definition: Written documents; academic publications. Doctors read medical papers about AA.

partnership.definition: Joining together for a common purpose; mutual cooperation. How groups function in AA.

patience.definition: Tolerance of delay or difficulty; calm endurance. Needed in amends work and daily recovery.

peace.definition: Freedom from war or violence; state of tranquility. Result of spiritual principles in action.

perception.definition: Awareness or understanding; way of viewing. New perception of life.

perfection.definition: State of being perfect; completeness. Not required but continuous improvement is.

perseverance.definition: Continued effort despite difficulty; persistence in recovery work. Required for Step Four inventory.

personalities.definition: Individual qualities or characteristics. AA places principles before personalities.

persons harmed.definition: Individual people who have been injured or wronged by one's actions. The focus of Step Eight amends work.

perspective.definition: Particular way of viewing things; viewpoint. Changed perspective through Steps.

physicians.definition: Doctors; medical practitioners. Used metaphorically for AA members helping sick alcoholics.

picture.definition: A visual representation or moving picture. Seeing AA in movies brings inquiries.

policy.definition: A course of action or principle adopted. AA's public relations policy is based on attraction.

poverty.definition: The state of being poor; lack of money or resources. Poverty will disappear in the promised future.

Power greater than ourselves.definition: The concept that some force outside individual willpower can help solve addiction. Does not require belief in God - can be community support, natural law, human interconnection, or any source of strength beyond oneself.

powerless.definition: Unable to control something through one's own strength; admitting inability to overcome alcoholism alone.

powerlessness.definition: The condition of being unable to control alcohol through willpower or self-effort; the fundamental truth of Step One.

prayer.definition: A respectful request or petition to a higher power; spiritual communication. Central to Step Eleven.

precarious.definition: Risky and unstable; easily lost or broken. Sobriety without spiritual change is precarious and fragile.

precedent.definition: Earlier event used as example for future action. AA's history provides precedent.

presentation.definition: Way in which something is presented. Presentation of the Steps.

president.definition: Leader of an organization. AA has no president with governing authority.

press.definition: News media; newspapers and journalists. Anonymity must be maintained before the press.

prestige.definition: Widespread respect and admiration; high status. Problems of money and prestige can divert from primary purpose.

primary.definition: First in order of importance; principal. AA's primary purpose is carrying the message.

primary purpose.definition: The main or fundamental goal. AA's primary purpose is to carry the message to the alcoholic who still suffers.

primordial.definition: From the earliest times; ancient or primitive. References the primordial ooze of evolutionary theory.

principle.definition: A fundamental truth or rule. In AA, principles are truths like honesty, humility, and faith that guide how to live.

priority.definition: Something regarded as more important. Sobriety and others come before self.

professional class.definition: A group of paid professionals. AA will never have a professional class of paid workers.

profit.definition: Financial gain or benefit; advantage. Spiritual profit from principles.

program.definition: A planned course of action or broadcast. Radio programs bring people to AA.

progress.definition: Forward movement toward a goal; advancement. Making progress through Steps.

promotion.definition: Advertising or marketing to advance something. AA's policy avoids promotion.

proposition.definition: A proposed plan or course of action; a statement for consideration. The proposition that God can remove character defects.

prospects.definition: Potential members; people likely to join. New prospects could find early AA through trusted friends.

prosperity.definition: State of being successful and wealthy; flourishing. Spiritual prosperity through recovery.

protection.definition: Act of protecting or defending. Protection of recovery through principles.

proved.definition: Demonstrated to be true through evidence or experience; verified. AA's principles are proved by countless recoveries.

providence.definition: Divine guidance, care, or intervention; fate ordained by God. References God's role in recovery.

prudence.definition: Caution and careful judgment; wisdom in practical affairs. One of the qualities needed for Step Nine.

public controversy.definition: Public debate or disagreement about disputed matters. AA should never be drawn into public controversy.

publication.definition: The act of making something public; a published work. The Big Book's publication in 1939.

publicity.definition: Public attention or awareness. Favorable publicity has brought alcoholics into AA.

purpose.definition: The reason for which something is done; intention. A useful purpose in life comes from spiritual principles.

quality.definition: A distinctive attribute or characteristic. Humility is a precious quality needed for true happiness.

radio.definition: Broadcast medium; radio programs. Anonymity must be maintained in radio appearances.

rapacious.definition: Greedily and aggressively seeking wealth or possessions; voracious. In the text, alcohol is described as a 'rapacious creditor.'

rationality.definition: Quality of being rational; sound reasoning. Restored sanity and rationality.

reality.definition: State of things as they actually exist. Facing reality rather than denial.

reason.definition: Cause or basis for something; rational thought. Using reason in recovery.

reasoning.definition: Process of thinking logically; argumentation. Sound reasoning in recovery.

recovery.definition: Return to normal health or state; restoration. The process of becoming well through the Twelve Steps.

refuge.definition: Safe place offering protection. AA as refuge from alcoholism.

regard.definition: Consideration and concern for someone. Having regard for those harmed.

reliability.definition: Quality of being reliable; dependable. Being reliable in recovery.

relief.definition: The removal or alleviation of pain, distress, or burden. Also used in legal/geographic contexts.

reluctance.definition: Unwillingness or resistance. Reluctance to admit wrongs is a major obstacle to recovery.

renounce.definition: To formally declare one's rejection or abandonment of something. What one must do to accept a Higher Power.

reproduction.definition: Procreation; the biological drive to have offspring. One of the God-given instincts necessary for humanity.

requirement.definition: Something demanded or essential; a necessary condition. The only requirement for AA membership is desire to stop drinking.

resentment.definition: Prolonged anger over something that feels unfair. In Step Four, resentments are examined to find the underlying fear or threat beneath the anger.

reservations.definition: Mental hesitations or doubts; also means areas set aside. Taking Step Six without reservations means complete willingness.

resources.definition: Assets or supplies available for use. Immense resources promise material abundance.

respect.definition: Regard or consideration for someone; esteem. All members deserve respect.

responsibility.definition: Duty or obligation; accountability. AA bears responsibility for good public relations.

restored to sanity.definition: Recovery of the ability to think clearly about alcohol and make sober decisions. For many, this means freedom from the mental obsession to drink despite devastating consequences.

restraint.definition: Holding yourself back from acting on impulse. Control over your own behavior.

revelation.definition: Disclosure of something hidden; divine disclosure. Spiritual revelation.

reward.definition: Something given in return for good behavior. The reward of recovery.

root.definition: Source or origin; underlying cause. Root cause of alcoholism.

sacrifice.definition: Giving up something valued. The sacrificial spirit - willingness to give up self-interest for the group - is the foundation of all traditions.

safety.definition: Condition of being safe; freedom from danger. Safety in fellowship.

sanctuary.definition: Sacred place offering safety or protection. AA as sanctuary.

sanity.definition: In AA, restored to sanity means freedom from the mental obsession about drinking (a narrower use of the word than general mental health). It doesn't require being declared legally insane - just that drinking decisions are made rationally, not through obsessive thinking.

satisfaction.definition: Fulfillment of a desire; contentment. Satisfaction comes from service and connection to others, not from external achievements or possessions.

schools.definition: Educational institutions. AA considered starting its own schools but should not.

science.definition: Knowledge of the natural world gained through observation and experimentation.

searching.definition: Going deep and honest; not skimming surface issues but looking beneath resentments to find root causes. In Step Four, searching means writing thoroughly until patterns become clear.

searchlight.definition: A light that scans a wide area. Used metaphorically for the searching examination of Step Four.

secrets.definition: Information kept hidden or unknown. Nature's secrets being forced into disclosure.

security.definition: The state of being safe and protected; freedom from fear. Both emotional and material security are legitimate desires.

self-awareness.definition: Conscious knowledge of one's own existence and nature. Developed through inventory work.

self-centered fear.definition: Anxiety based on the belief that you must control everything to feel safe. It's not rational fear (like fear of fire), but the nervous certainty that bad things will happen unless things go exactly your way.

self-control.definition: Control of one's emotions and actions. Developed through Steps.

self-discipline.definition: Control over one's own behavior and desires. Required for recovery.

self-examination.definition: Critical inspection of one's own thoughts and actions. Part of Step Four inventory.

self-knowledge.definition: Understanding of one's own nature and characteristics. Gained through Step Four.

self-reliance.definition: Reliance on one's own resources; independence. Must be balanced with reliance on Higher Power.

self-sufficiency.definition: The ability to provide for oneself without help from others. Alcoholism destroys self-sufficiency and self-reliance.

self-supporting.definition: Able to support oneself financially without outside help. AA groups must be fully self-supporting.

selflessness.definition: Concern for others rather than oneself; absence of self-centeredness. Opposite of alcoholic self-obsession.

serenity.definition: The state of being calm and peaceful. The goal of living serenely today and tomorrow through Step Ten.

servants.definition: Those who serve others' interests. AA leaders function as servants, not governors.

service.definition: Helpful activity directed toward another; working for others' benefit. In AA, service means helping at meetings, sponsoring others, or holding group positions - but also the principle of 'anonymous service' where you help without recognition. Service is how recovered alcoholics 'carry the message.'

service boards.definition: Groups designated to perform specific functions. AA may create service boards responsible to those they serve.

sex relation.definition: Sexual relationship between people. In AA context, one of the God-given instincts that must be properly directed.

shelter.definition: A building providing protection from weather. Basic human need requiring instinctual drive and effort.

shortcomings.definition: Failures to reach a standard; imperfections or flaws. Step Seven asks for removal of shortcomings.

sick people.definition: People with the disease of alcoholism. How AA came to view alcoholics - as sick, not criminal.

significance.definition: Importance or notability; meaning. The significance of the Steps.

sincerity.definition: Genuine feeling or intention; honest and genuine. Required in all Steps.

sisterhood.definition: Feminine version of brotherhood; female fellowship and kinship. Part of AA's inclusive community.

skid rows.name: skid rows
skid rows.definition: Urban areas with cheap housing and typically associated with poverty, homelessness, and heavy drinking.

slip.definition: A return to drinking after sobriety. The terms are distinguished - 'slip' may be brief, 'relapse' is sustained. Both indicate need to revisit Steps or increase fellowship.

sober.definition: Free from alcohol; mentally clear and emotionally balanced; living according to AA principles.

sobriety.definition: The state of abstaining from alcohol; also means clarity of mind and emotional balance in recovery.

social instinct.definition: The innate drive to live in groups and form communities. When misdirected, causes social problems.

societies.definition: Organized groups of people. Early AA groups were secret societies.

society.definition: The aggregate of people living together in an organized community. Requires social instinct for existence.

Society.name: Society
Society.definition: Another term for the AA fellowship or the collective body of AA groups and members.

sole authority.definition: Single, exclusive power. God is the sole authority in AA through group conscience.

solidity.definition: Quality of being solid; substance. Solid foundation in fellowship.

soundness.definition: Quality of being sound; integrity. Sound recovery program.

source.definition: Origin or starting point. Source of recovery is Higher Power.

spearhead.definition: The front or leading position of an attack or movement; to lead. Man as the spearhead of evolution.

specialty.definition: A particular area of expertise. Each physician has a specialty but must focus on the common cure.

spirit.definition: The essential nature; the attitude or mood. The sacrificial spirit symbolized by anonymity.

spiritual awakening.definition: An awakening to spiritual reality and connection with a power greater than self. The result of working the Steps.

spiritual direction.definition: Guidance toward spiritual growth and awakening. Part of Step Eleven practice.

spiritual experience.definition: A moment of clarity, connection, or transformation. In AA, can be dramatic conversion or quiet realization. Not necessarily religious - can be insight from group support, nature, meditation, or any profound human experience.

spiritual not religious.definition: The Steps are built on core principles (honesty, humility, faith) rather than requiring membership in any specific religion. You can be spiritual through nature, science, ethics, or helping others - without joining a church.

spiritually.definition: Relating to the spirit or soul; concerning spiritual matters. Growing spiritually through practicing the Steps.

sponsor.definition: A person further along in recovery who guides another through the Steps and offers support during difficulty. Not a therapist, but an experienced peer who has worked the program.

stability.definition: Quality of being stable; firmness. Stable recovery foundation.

stark.definition: Bare, severe, or harsh; starkly obvious. The stark fact of alcoholism must be accepted.

steadiness.definition: Quality of being steady and stable. Steady progress in recovery.

step.definition: One of the 12 progressive spiritual and behavioral practices that form the foundation of AA recovery. The complete set is called 'the Twelve Steps' or 'the Steps.'

Step Five.name: Step Five
Step Five.definition: The fifth of the Twelve Steps: 'Admitted to God, to ourselves, and to another human being the exact nature of our wrongs.'

Step Four.name: Step Four
Step Four.definition: The fourth of the Twelve Steps: 'Made a searching and fearless moral inventory of ourselves.'

Step One.name: Step One
Step One.definition: The first of the Twelve Steps: 'We admitted we were powerless over alcohol - that our lives had become unmanageable.'

Step Seven.name: Step Seven
Step Seven.definition: The seventh of the Twelve Steps: 'Humbly asked Him to remove our shortcomings.'

Step Six.name: Step Six
Step Six.definition: The sixth of the Twelve Steps: 'Were entirely ready to have God remove all these defects of character.'

Step Three.name: Step Three
Step Three.definition: The third of the Twelve Steps: 'Made a decision to turn our will and our lives over to the care of God, as we understood Him.'

Step Two.name: Step Two
Step Two.definition: The second of the Twelve Steps: 'Came to believe that a Power greater than ourselves could restore us to sanity.'

still suffering.definition: The phrase 'the alcoholic who still suffers' refers to anyone still caught in active addiction or seeking recovery. AA's commitment to reach these people motivates all service work.

strength.definition: Physical or mental power; resilience and capacity to endure. Comes from admitting defeat and relying on Higher Power.

substance.definition: The essential nature of something. The spiritual substance of anonymity is sacrifice.

success.definition: Accomplishment of desired aim; prosperous achievement. Success in recovery.

sufferer.definition: One who endures pain or hardship. The alcoholic who still suffers is AA's primary purpose.

sufficiency.definition: Having enough of what you need; being self-sufficient. The ability to provide for yourself.

support.definition: Giving assistance or backing; helping. Support for recovery and members.

surrender.definition: Giving up resistance and the illusion of control. In AA, surrender means accepting that you cannot manage your drinking alone and must rely on a Power greater than yourself. It's not weakness but honesty - letting go of the fight so recovery can begin.

survey.definition: A systematic examination or inspection. Taking a survey of the harm caused to others.

survival.definition: Remaining alive; basic human need. Competition for survival drives social and economic behavior.

symbolized.definition: Represented by a symbol. Anonymity symbolizes the sacrificial spirit.

take sides.definition: To choose one position over another in a dispute. AA must never take sides in any fight.

talk.definition: Conversation or spoken words. Much everyday talk highlights pride in human achievement.

telephones.definition: Communication devices. Telephones ring constantly in AA with inquiries.

television.definition: Electronic medium for broadcasting. TV coverage brings people to AA.

temperance.definition: Moderation and restraint; not excessive. Balanced living in recovery.

tested principles.definition: Principles that have been proven effective through experience. What groups must conform to for survival.

thinking.definition: Mental activity; reasoning and beliefs. Wrong thinking causes hurt to oneself and others.

thriving.definition: Prospering and succeeding; flourishing. Healthy members thrive in AA.

timing.definition: The choice of when to do something. Good judgment about timing is essential in Step Nine.

tolerance.definition: Acceptance of different beliefs or behaviors; lack of prejudice. AA welcomes all who desire sobriety.

tormenting.definition: Causing severe pain, suffering, or anguish. The emotional ghosts of the past torment alcoholics.

tradition.definition: Long-established practice or custom; inherited belief system. AA's 'Twelve Traditions' are the principles that guide how groups function and relate to each other.

Tradition Nine.name: Tradition Nine
Tradition Nine.definition: The ninth of the Twelve Traditions: 'A.A., as such, ought never be organized; but we may create service boards or committees directly responsible to those they serve.'

Tradition One.name: Tradition One
Tradition One.definition: The first of the Twelve Traditions: 'Our common welfare should come first; personal recovery depends upon A.A. unity.'

Tradition Two.name: Tradition Two
Tradition Two.definition: The second of the Twelve Traditions: 'For our group purpose there is but one ultimate authority - a loving God as He may express Himself in our group conscience.'

transparency.definition: Quality of being transparent; clarity. Transparency in amends.

treasurer.definition: One who manages funds. AA has no treasurer to compel payment of dues.

treasury.definition: The funds or financial resources of an organization. Every AA group should establish a permanent treasury.

trouble.definition: Difficulty, distress, or problems. Misdirected instincts cause all kinds of human trouble.

trust.definition: Confidence in the reliability of another person or Higher Power. Essential to sponsor relationships and faith.

trust in God.definition: Confidence in a Higher Power's care and guidance. Central to Steps Two and Three.

truth.definition: Accordance with fact or reality; honesty. Basis of all AA work and recovery.

turned it over.definition: Stopped trying to control something through willpower and instead trusted it to a Higher Power. Central action in Step Three and throughout recovery.

twelfth-stepper.definition: A paid professional attempting recovery work. Alcoholics will not listen to a paid twelfth-stepper.

Twelve Steps.name: Twelve Steps
Twelve Steps.definition: The complete set of 12 spiritual principles that form the recovery program of Alcoholics Anonymous.

Twelve Traditions.name: Twelve Traditions
Twelve Traditions.definition: The 12 principles that guide how AA groups function and maintain unity, focusing on the fellowship's survival and principles.

tyrannize.definition: To rule or treat with cruelty and oppression; to dominate. How unchecked instincts tyrannize alcoholics.

U.S.A..name: U.S.A.
U.S.A..definition: United States of America; indicates the location of the example.

unconditional surrender.definition: Giving up the fight to control everything; accepting powerlessness over people, places, and things. Paradoxically, this surrender brings freedom and peace.

understanding.definition: Knowledge or grasp of something. Understanding the Steps.

unique ability.definition: A special or distinctive capacity. Each AA member has unique ability to identify with alcoholics.

unity.definition: The state of being united; togetherness. AA's survival depends on unity among members.

unmanageable.definition: Unable to be managed or controlled; chaotic. Describes the lives of alcoholics before recovery.

value.definition: Importance or usefulness; principles one holds dear. Core values in recovery.

vanished.definition: Disappeared suddenly or completely; ceased to exist. The obsession to drink vanished when Step Six work was done.

virtue.definition: Moral excellence; a particular moral quality. Development of virtues through Steps.

warped.definition: Twisted or bent out of shape; distorted. Describes how alcoholism twists the mind.

way of life.definition: One's manner of living; lifestyle. Humility as a way of life is rare in modern culture.

weakness.definition: Lack of strength or power. Acknowledging one's weakness is first step to recovery.

welfare.definition: Health and happiness; well-being. Common welfare must come before personal interests in AA.

well-being.definition: State of being comfortable and healthy; welfare. Emotional well-being through recovery.

well-wishers.definition: Those who wish someone well; supporters. The many people who help AA grow.

wellness.definition: State of being in good health; overall well-being. Goal of AA recovery program.

wholeness.definition: Completeness; being unbroken or undivided. 'We stay whole, or A.A. dies.'

willing.definition: Ready, prepared, or consenting. In AA, willingness is the willingness to listen to suggestions, willingness to believe, willingness to change - even if you have doubts. It's not about enthusiasm but about opening yourself to try.

willingness.definition: Openness and readiness to take action; one of the most important qualities in AA recovery.

willpower.definition: The strength of will to carry out one's decisions; self-discipline. Willpower alone cannot overcome alcoholism.

wisdom.definition: Deep understanding and good judgment based on experience. Gained through Step work.

worked the Steps.definition: Gone through all 12 Steps with a sponsor, doing the written work and sharing results with another person. A major commitment taking months or longer, not to be rushed.

worth.definition: Deserving of something; having value. Recovering worth through recovery.

wreckage.definition: Destruction; the ruins or damage left behind. The human damage alcoholism causes.

wrongs.definition: Unjust or harmful acts; violations of right conduct. Step Five involves admitting the exact nature of one's wrongs.

got us over the barrel.definition: Trapped us in an impossible position with no good options (a common expression meaning someone is helpless or cornered). In the 12x12, AA acknowledges it's trapped newcomers: admitting powerlessness over alcohol, then saying only a Power greater than yourself can help.

hoop you have to jump through.word: hoop you have to jump through
hoop you have to jump through.definition: A difficult requirement or daunting process (originally from circus animals jumping through hoops). In Step Two, the sponsor says believing in a Higher Power may seem like an impossible hoop to jump through, but it's much wider than the newcomer fears.

bogeymen.definition: Scary phantoms or imaginary fears that seem very real and threatening but aren't (from folklore about creatures used to frighten children). In Step Four, pride and fear feel like towering barriers, but AA says they're bogeymen - once you actually start the inventory, they lose all their power.

just about the last straw.definition: The final thing that breaks your patience or hope; the moment when you can't take anymore. Newcomers feel the Step Two requirement is "just about the last straw" after being told they're powerless.

beginning of the end.definition: A turning point where an old way of life is ending and something new is beginning. Used to show that the sponsor's laugh signals the start of the end of the newcomer's old life and the start of their recovery.

restless irritable discontented.definition: A state of mind common in alcoholics even when not drinking - feeling unsettled, annoyed, and unsatisfied with life. These feelings are why alcoholics want to escape through drinking.

allergy.definition: In AA, a physical abnormality in how the alcoholic's body reacts to alcohol (borrowed from medical terminology for immune overreaction). Like a shellfish allergy - where the body reacts badly to something harmless to others - an alcoholic's body can never safely tolerate alcohol, even in small amounts. Combined with mental obsession, it makes recovery impossible without outside help.

restore to sanity.definition: To lift the mental obsession about drinking and restore the ability to think clearly. Sanity in AA means freedom from the obsessive thinking about alcohol - not needing to be declared insane, but being freed from the crazy idea that you can control your drinking.

two-part illness.definition: AA's view that alcoholism has two inseparable parts: mental obsession (the constant thinking about drinking) and physical allergy (the body's uncontrollable craving). Neither part alone explains alcoholism; both must be understood.

controlled drinking.definition: The hope that an alcoholic can drink "normally" or in moderation. AA says this is impossible for true alcoholics - the allergy and obsession make control impossible no matter how much willpower is used.

will.definition: Personal desire, determination, or choice. In AA, often distinguished from willpower (which cannot solve alcoholism alone) and from God's will (which Step Three asks us to follow). Step One distinguishes between resisting alcohol through willpower and accepting powerlessness. Step Three is about turning 'our will and our lives over' to a Higher Power.

power.definition: Strength or ability; in AA, often refers to 'a Power greater than ourselves' - the Higher Power. Distinct from personal power or willpower (which alcoholics lack over alcohol). AA teaches that recovery requires relying on this greater Power, not on individual strength or determination.
